feedback.text: The Stop Destroying Games initiative is a relatively small issue that shares a lot of similarities adjacent to this act. Large players within the industry like to sell products under a license rather than a good and this is not limited to video games. Services from Apple, Playstation or Amazon to name a few where you 'bought' products such as games, movies, music end up just being glorified rentals rather than actual purchases. With no specific end date and the item can just be pulled at the whim of the seller from the user's library. Since the consumer has purchased a license, not a good there should be a clear distinction here. Do I buy a good and own it, or do I rent a product and accept it can be pulled from the store and my library at any given time? Traditionally in the EU, there was a clear distinction. Buying meant ownership. But since the landscape has changed and companies muddied the waters by changing the definition of ownership this has to be re-evaluated as well. A company should not be able to mandate me to destroy something I bought physically in stores in an EULA shown after the purchase which is non binding in EU should the publisher ever decide so. Smartphones and other relatively new developments produced by big tech put current consumer law to the test and completely ignores consumer agency. Smartphones are being used as the ultimate tracking device where respect for privacy is gone. At the same time, Google has removed support for their phones that allows users to run custom software (AOSP). Apple has been forced to open up by the EU. But the massive data hoard trend has been picked up by many others and anyone not tech savvy enough will be incapable of any self agency who value privacy and are at the mercy of big tech. Since privacy matters to me, I have gone to great lengths to minimize data leaking to companies that seek to benefit from selling that data. I am the owner of a device, I should be able to use it however I want. But there is too much malicious compliance happening in this space by not giving the tools to do so, or at worst, removing the tools previously given.
